skill.name: airunway-aks-setup
skill.description: Guide an AI assistant through AI Runway setup on an existing AKS cluster, including cluster checks, controller installation, GPU assessment, provider setup, and first model deployment.
skill.summary: Set up AI Runway on AKS from cluster verification to a working model deployment.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, network, and weak-crypto patterns, but review shows most are Markdown command examples for AKS administration, not hidden executable code. The confirmed risk is operational: the skill asks an assistant to run cluster-changing kubectl and make commands, create a Kubernetes secret from a HuggingFace token, and call a model endpoint. No evidence of prompt injection, malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or obfuscated payloads was found.

medium_findings.title: Cluster-Modifying Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run kubectl and make commands that install CRDs, deploy controllers, deploy inference providers, apply model resources, and inspect cluster workloads. These actions are legitimate for the skill purpose, but they can change production AKS state and incur cloud cost if run without careful confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit Kubernetes administration operations and several modify cluster resources. The surrounding instructions require user confirmation before install or deployment, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Token Secret Handling
medium_findings.description: The deployment flow asks the user for a HuggingFace token, writes it to a temporary file, and creates a Kubernetes secret. The Bash path uses mktemp, chmod, trap cleanup, and unsets the variable, but the token still becomes a cluster secret that requires normal Kubernetes secret controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: There is direct evidence of credential handling and secret creation. The implementation guidance includes reasonable local cleanup, but Kubernetes secret exposure remains a real security consideration.

low_findings.title: Network Smoke Test Is User-Controlled
low_findings.description: The skill retrieves a model endpoint from Kubernetes status and sends a simple chat-completions smoke test. This is expected model-serving validation, but users should verify the endpoint before sending any real prompts or data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network call is explicit and tied to a deployment endpoint, not an unknown exfiltration host. The endpoint value is dynamic, so a human should still confirm it before use.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings map to ordinary AI and GPU documentation text such as bfloat16, model names, and table content. No evidence of cryptographic implementation, hashing, encryption, or downgrade behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation and command text, not crypto code. The scanner appears to have matched substrings in prose and Markdown tables.

low_findings.title: Markdown Relative Links Flagged As Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal findings are relative Markdown links to nearby reference documents, such as powershell-notes.md and gpu-profiles.md. They do not read arbitrary filesystem paths or construct paths from user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation links, not filesystem APIs. No user-controlled path traversal or file disclosure path was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Administrative Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many Bash and PowerShell examples for kubectl, make, curl, and provider deployment. These examples are expected for AKS setup, but marketplace users should treat them as privileged administrative actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present in Markdown code blocks and are central to the skill. They are not auto-executing payloads, but they can change cloud infrastructure when followed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Temporary Secret File Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The Bash and PowerShell token workflows temporarily place a HuggingFace token on disk before creating a Kubernetes secret. Cleanup is included, but local file permissions and cluster secret access controls remain important.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token flow is plainly documented and uses mitigations. The remaining risk is exposure through local temp storage or Kubernetes secret access.

content.user_title: Set Up AI Runway on AKS
content.value_statement: Teams need a repeatable way to bring AI Runway online without missing cluster, GPU, provider, or model steps. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through verification, installation, deployment, and validation on AKS.
actual_capabilities: Checks AKS context, node inventory, and GPU availability before setup.
Guides AI Runway controller and CRD installation from the repository root.
Maps detected GPU types to dtype, attention backend, and model sizing guidance.
Recommends KAITO, Dynamo, or KubeRay based on hardware and serving needs.
Creates first ModelDeployment manifests for gated and non-gated starter models.
Provides Bash and PowerShell variants for key setup and validation commands.
limitations: Requires an existing AKS cluster and local access to kubectl, make, and curl.
Does not provision the AKS cluster or GPU node pools by itself.
Commands can change cluster state and may create cloud compute costs.
Model recommendations depend on current provider support and available GPU capacity.

use_cases.title: Onboard a Platform Cluster
use_cases.description: Verify an existing AKS cluster, install AI Runway components, and confirm provider readiness.

use_cases.title: Launch a Starter Model
use_cases.description: Select a model that fits available GPU memory and deploy it through a ModelDeployment resource.

use_cases.title: Validate GPU Serving Readiness
use_cases.description: Check GPU compatibility, dtype constraints, endpoint readiness, and rollback commands before broader rollout.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Cluster
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the airunway-aks-setup skill to verify my current AKS context, node inventory, and GPU availability. Do not install anything yet.

prompt_templates.title: Install AI Runway Controller
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the airunway-aks-setup skill from step 2. Check whether the controller is already installed, then ask before running any install commands.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Provider and Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the airunway-aks-setup skill to assess my GPU profile, recommend an inference provider, and suggest a starter model that fits my cluster.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy and Validate Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the airunway-aks-setup skill from step 5 to deploy a confirmed model, monitor readiness, run a smoke test, and summarize rollback steps.

output_examples.input: Verify my cluster before AI Runway setup.
output_examples.output: A concise readiness report listing current context, node count, GPU types, missing tools, and whether setup can continue.

output_examples.input: Recommend a model for one L4 GPU.
output_examples.output: A recommendation that explains expected memory fit, provider choice, dtype constraints, and whether a HuggingFace token is needed.
A deployment summary with controller status, provider readiness, model status, endpoint validation result, and next operational steps.

best_practices: Run verification commands first and confirm the target cluster context before installation.
Ask for explicit approval before controller, provider, model, or rollback actions.
Use least-privilege Kubernetes access and rotate tokens used for gated model downloads.
anti_patterns: Do not run setup against an unknown or production cluster without confirming context.
Do not paste HuggingFace tokens directly into shell history or shared logs.
Do not deploy large GPU models before checking VRAM, dtype support, and cost impact.

faq.question: Does this skill create an AKS cluster?
faq.answer: No. It assumes an AKS cluster already exists and focuses on AI Runway setup and model deployment.

faq.question: Can it work without GPUs?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents a CPU-only path using KAITO with llama.cpp and a small quantized model.

faq.question: Does it support Windows users?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes PowerShell command variants for key Bash-specific setup and validation steps.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it guides privileged cluster changes, secret creation, and endpoint testing.

faq.question: What tools must be installed locally?
faq.answer: The skill expects kubectl, make, and curl, plus access to the AI Runway repository root.

faq.question: How are gated models handled?
faq.answer: The skill prompts for a HuggingFace token and creates a Kubernetes secret for models that require access approval.
